speaker: interviewee
text: --how long we marched. But we started realizing that we were on the outskirts of a little city. And we for sure knew we were near the River Dniester.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being that it was a very large river, and people started to find out where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they started maybe asking some of the Romanians. I don't know. But word started spreading that we were going to Ataki. And we knew that Ataki was on the-- on the River Dniester. And sure enough, I think by midday we marched into this town, to this shtetl where again we encountered empty streets and empty houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that march?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been several hours. It must've been from maybe 6, 7 o'clock in the morning until 10, 11 o'clock. I don't remember for sure. But I do remember that as we were getting into the-- into town, people started marching towards houses. And this house was empty. And that house was empty. And so we marched into a house-- my dad, and my mom, and my brother Joe, and myself. House was empty. We thought it was empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there furniture or anything in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was-- not, not orderly. It was like they were all been looted. So there was just the odd something or other. But it wasn't like a furnished home which was ready for occupation. It was-- we understood that the people must have been evacuated or marched out of town-- the local Jewish people. It was Jewish people because we had mezuzahs on the doors and messages on the walls. So we realized that those people must have been taken out of their homes. And of course, after they were gone, everything was looted. But in the house we were in, there wasn't very much, just the four walls. But on the walls and on the doors, in Yiddish and in whatever language, messages. And the messages were loud and clear. First thing, say kaddish after us. Don't forget us. Tell the world what happened to us-- everywhere. That message stays with me. I'll carry it. And I'll pass it on. [CRYING] We owe it to those people that obviously were taken out of their homes and murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so again, we were revealed that we didn't know what, what happened. Why did they killed those people? Where did they kill them? And what's going to happen to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were tired. We were hungry. We were frightened. Kids at that age don't realize so much the danger as the older people. Kids somehow don't feel it. They seem to be insulated somehow of all these dangers. They're not confronted with the responsibility. They take it for granted that, yeah, it's a march. It's a game. It's a-- it'll-- it'll go on. I don't know. Maybe I was stupid. But that's how it was. We were frightened, of course. We knew that we don't have a home. We don't know where we are going. All of a sudden people are being shot, being killed. But somehow, we never really-- I wasn't afraid at that time yet. We bedded down for the night. We had whatever little food we shared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you bed down?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that house.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that house, in the empty house. Nobody ventured out to see what was going on outside. Everybody was tired, and tired, and beaten, and, and just wanted to sleep. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] I don't remember how long we stayed in Ataki. I do remember, though, that in the same house that we were in, there were several rooms. And my dad started to look around to see what was in the other rooms. And there were two people in one room on the floor covered with an old blanket. And my dad went up to see what-- who those people were. And they were alive, but they weren't in very good shape. And my dad recognized-- he recognized-- there was a man and woman. He recognized the man because that man was a large landowner and forest owner. And my dad, having been employed in the lumber industry and in the forest industry, recognized him because he was his employer. Or at least my dad was supervising a group of people that were in his employ.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't remember his name. But I do remember my dad saying that he was a relative of the Baron Hirsch. And they owned large tracts of land. And that man recognized my dad too and told him, [NON-ENGLISH]. Did  you see what happened to us? Where did he come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was probably taken like all the other people. I think he must've been in another small city, from somewhere. I, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was not from Vishnitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. Although he used to live-- he had his own living quarters outside of Vishnitz on a large estate. But I don't know. I'm not familiar with exactly where it was. But I do remember my dad always talking about, about that. We shared whatever food we had with those two people. And as I said, I don't remember how long we stayed in Ataki. It couldn't have been very long. It must've been a day or two. And then again the gendarmes started to take people out of their-- of these houses, marching them towards, towards the river. And as soon as a group assembled on the river, we were ferried across into what was the first city in the Ukraine which was to be Transnistria for us. The name of the city was Mohyliv-Podilskyi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the river?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Dniester. Later, of course, we found out that a lot of the first people that were in Yedinitz, as well as some people that lived in Ataki, when they arrived to Ataki, they were simply marched into the river and machine gunned without any thought or feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they were driven into--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were driven into the river and shot in the back with machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Romanians. And maybe some of the Ukrainians were helping them in the matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you taken across?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were ferried across.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was like a large boat. I remember it was a large boat. And in the boat, there was German soldiers. And we were there. And I remember the boat was loaded very heavy, because the water was just inches from the top. And we were scared. We were worried that we were going to drown. But we made it across. We made it across. And when we got across onto the other side--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that motorized? How did that move?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was by cable. It was by cable. As far as I remember, it was by cable. When we arrived on the other side, we were told to march in the direction of the town or the city. It was a kind of a main street. We said, go. Didn't know where to go. And it was starting to get dark. But people started to march. And we were looking for shelter. And there was a large building. I remember it was a large green building. And we ventured in into that building. And no sooner we got in, we realized that there were sentries at the doors. And you couldn't get out. And when we got in there, we realized that there were a lot of other people in that building, and also in one large, large room, a sight which we couldn't believe, which we were told and realized that they were from an insane asylum. It must've been maybe 50 or 60 inmates.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how were they different?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sight in that room was just unbelievable. These people were, were beating each other. There was blood all over. There was human refuse all over. They were smearing each other with that. They were laid out like, like wood, one to the next on the floor. This stench was unbelievable. I realized that we had to do whatever we could to get out of there. And I said to my dad, I said I'm going to see maybe, maybe, I can bribe the sentry at the door to let us out. And we also had with us in the same group my uncle on my mother's side, Uncle Litmann, and his wife and his two daughters, my cousins. Somehow, I don't know, we were lucky. And I managed to bribe the sentry. And he allowed all of us-- our family, four of us and four of them-- to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you give him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a watch. And I had some money. I don't remember exactly how much. I know that I gave him my watch. And I had some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Romanian money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I gave him, and he let us out. He let us out. And here we are in a strange city in the middle of quite dark, not knowing where we are or where-- what to do. But we were happy to be outside because that house was-- it was just impossible. We'd never, never imagined anything like that. And I remember we walked alongside the river and found a shack that was half full of sand because the river had gone over its banks that year. And it was in 1941, late October. It was getting cold at night. But we got into that shack. And we slept on the wet sand. Next morning, we got up, and we started to find some kind of shelter. And we finally did find shelter with some people in the city for which we had to pay for with whatever little we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you walk around freely from one street to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: People were walking around. People were communicating. Yeah. We were, of course-- we knew that we were under occupation. We knew that we were in danger. But we hadn't-- we didn't yet know what the extent of our danger was or what our next move was going to be. But my dad had made contact with some people. And they found out that our best bet is to leave Mohyliv-Podilskyi and march to another little town. And the name of Shargorod was mentioned. And we were told that Shargorod was a pretty good little community, and that we have a chance of maybe finding some accommodation there. And that's where-- that's what people started to talk about. And yes, on about the third or fourth day, a convoy was organized, supervised, of course, by the Romanians-- the gendarmes again. And the march started on foot, except that there were a couple of wagons for the belongings. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what made the Romanians take you where you-- to Shargorod?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Romanians were in charge of moving to people further in inland because new people were coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inland to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inland into the Ukraine, into Transnistria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they call that Transnistria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because it was on the other side of the River Dniester. And it was a territory given by the Germans, for, for-- to the-- promised to the Romanians because the Romanians had joined the war against the Russians. And the territory was designated between the River Dniester to the River Bug, and between Mohyliv all the way down, I believe, to Odessa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the territory. And the people that were arriving daily were being moved into Mohyliv-Podilskyi and moved out of Mohyliv-Podilskyi into different areas of Transnistria, or the Ukraine at that time. And later, of course, we found out they were many, many little ghettos.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we, we were marching not knowing really where we were going. But we heard Shargorod The people in that convoy were somewhat privileged because there were a lot of people from [NON-ENGLISH] who had better connections to the local authorities. And so we were lucky that we got on with that group. The first day, after 24 hours, I remember it was getting late in the day. Night was approaching. And we were approaching a village. And all the people in our group were marched into a stable, into a barn. It turned out to be a pig barn. But everybody was very tired. I remember my mother could no longer walk. So my brother and I, at the age of 13 and 14 kind of carried her on our shoulders for the last few kilometers until we got to the barn. If we hadn't carried her and she would have been left behind, she'd have probably been shot because anybody that couldn't walk, the Romanians didn't, didn't provide any transportation for. But we got into that barn. And everybody laid down on the cement or whatever ground there was there-- in the stench, in the wet. Again, I started to see whether maybe there was a chance to get out of that barn and find a better place. And lo and behold, we did, again, for my family and for my uncle's family. And we stayed that night with a local peasant who was rather nice to us. You know, we paid whatever-- I don't remember what we paid. And we gave him something we had. And he gave us a good accommodation. And they give us food. And they kind of felt sorry for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Ukrainian people. There were amongst the Ukrainian people many good people. Unfortunately, to every one good, there might have been 10 bad. But we were fortunate that night to find a good one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you as a 13, 14-year-old made so many important decisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I somehow took charge or took the initiative. Like I said, I was maybe at an age where I didn't, didn't, didn't fear danger as much as my parents did or the older people in the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your brother Joe, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12, one year younger than me. Or a year younger than me, 12 or 13. Anyway, the next day, we started our march again. And I believe we reached child Shargorod the next day towards evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many days did you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was two days, two days or a little better than two days, but two full days. We reached-- we reached Shargorod towards evening. We crossed a bridge. No sooner we crossed the bridge, there was a main highway. We took a left turn. And we were marched into what was a old synagogue, not very big. But that's where we landed the first-- towards evening. It was just getting dark. And not everybody maybe landed in that synagogue. But my family, and my uncle's family, and some other-- many other people landed in that little synagogue. And like I said, it was just getting dark. So I said to my father and mother, I'm going to see-- I'm going to take my youngest brother Joe with me, and we're going to go out to see maybe we can find somebody that will take us in for the night because the rumor was that we were going to be marching the next day again. Not everybody was going to be staying in Shargorod.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left the synagogue. And we only walked maybe what you would call in a city a large block. I don't know how many feet, maybe 400, 500, 600 feet from the synagogue. And here we are, my brother and myself holding hands in this dark. We don't know where  we're going. And all of a sudden, we see a man approaching us with a cane-- tall man with a mustache. He took a look at us. And he said, kinderlekh, where are you going? So we told him that we had just arrived with a convoy into the shul. And our parents are in the shul. And we didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: That man's name was Ester Hanach. That man [SOBBING] saved our lives. He went-- he said to us, come with me. I'll take you into my house. And I'll go and get your parents. And don't worry, I'll get them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he from a deportee or a local?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Jewish man from the local Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Ukraine. He went back and got my mother and father out of the shul and brought them in the house. It was a little house. I think it had three rooms, two and a half rooms. In that little house at that time, there were already seven or eight people-- some of their family and one other immigrant as well. I say immigrant, or refugee or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Deportee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Deportee. His name was Simon. I don't remember his second name. But he was from Romania. They took him in. And then they took us in. So here we are in those little rooms-- I don't remember if something like 12 people. And they were just, you know, they felt sorry for two young children walking in the middle of the night. So they took us into their house. And it was going to be for a few days-- ended up being for three and a half years. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And so we settled in to our new home. We didn't need much space. We had a corner where somehow we made a-- it wasn't a bed, and it wasn't a bench. It was a place where a few inches off the ground from plain planks, which would allow four people to sleep like one next to another. And that was our--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a special name for that kind of an arrangement?

speaker: interviewee
text: If there was, I don't know. For us, it was just our place where we lived for the next three and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the name [INAUDIBLE] familiar to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never heard the word. That was a-- so and then we, we were waiting for our belongings. Our belongings were following us. They were supposed to be sent to us because we couldn't take everything we had with us. And they had arrived after a short period of time. And at first, life in the what we found out was going to be the ghetto that we were going to live was kind of a matter of settling in. We didn't know where-- what was going to happen again, of course. But there was food available. The local Ukrainian population was allowed into the ghetto twice a week with food. And if you had something to barter, you could barter for food. And that's how we started to live-- whatever belongings we had, you know, we bartered and managed to survive. The first year, '41-- the winter of '41 was harsh. But we had enough belongings to barter. So we didn't go real hungry. And we lived in fear. But we soon started to hear about people starving, people dying of diseases. There were no sanitary facilities. There was the odd beating and killing by either Romanians or by some Ukrainian fascists. But it wasn't on a large scale. And we managed that winter to go through not in too bad of a shape.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you use for heating the huts-- those huts?

speaker: interviewee
text: The stoves were heated with, with wood and with oil. They used to press [NON-ENGLISH]-- the sunflower seeds, they used to press. And so they had-- I think they call it [NON-ENGLISH]. If I'm not mistaken, they used to use that for fuel too. And somehow, we, we managed to, to get used to living 12 people in a small house. It's a funny thing. Sometime two or three people need a large house, and they never have enough room. Here we were 12, old and young, and two little rooms with hardly any room to walk. But we realized that that's how it is. And the people were good enough to put up with us. And as I said before, we owe our life to those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there water there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water was available. It had to be carried from about, oh, maybe a kilometer. And we used to go for that. We used to carry water. As a matter of fact, carrying water became a profession after a certain length of time because if you went, and you endangered your life by going to the well and made it back, you maybe, maybe got a half a mark or some token for bringing the water. But it was available. It wasn't-- in the city or in the town, I don't remember of any water being right in the town. But I do remember going for water a long distance out of the-- out of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: The winter went, and we soon realized that if we continue on doing what we were doing, we were going to run out of belongings to barter. And fear started setting in. What are we going to live on when our belongings are gone? And we realized that we had to do something more than what we were doing in order to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Having had an agricultural background or an exposure to other than city life, because my grandparents were both landowners, we had to come to a decision. And we made a decision, rather than sit in town and resign to dying of starvation, we had to venture out in the countryside to see whether we could work to get enough for our work-- working the land, work whatever was available to get some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made that decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: It comes kind of by itself. It-- as I said, having been from the countryside, and having faced a situation that we have to survive, we had-- we weren't as much afraid as city people to venture into the countryside. We could speak the language because our Ukrainian people spoke the same language. And so it was a little easier for us to make that decision and to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If you would ever been caught by the authorities, they could either shoot you, or beat you up, or do whatever they wanted. But if you're hungry, and it's a matter of life and death, you do what you have to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. This is a good place to stop.
